title: Exceptional points in diamond optomechanics
authors: Waleed El-Sayed, Elham Zohari, Joe Itoi, Peyman Parsa, Gustavo de Oliveira Luiz, Joseph E. Losby, Misa Hayashida, Marek Malac, Paul E. Barclay
abstract: Multimode cavity optomechanical systems allow light to couple otherwise non-interacting mechanical resonators, enabling non-Hermitian phenomena such as exceptional points, where eigenfrequencies and eigenvectors of coupled modes coalesce. Accessing an exceptional point and its nearby parameter space is a first step towards chiral mode dynamics and topological state transfer. Diamond optomechanical devices support strong coherent optomechanical coupling required to tune resonances to an exceptional point, as well as strain-coupling to spin-defects for hybrid quantum technologies, but have not yet been used for multimode non-Hermitian physics. Here we tune to an exceptional point in a diamond optomechanical crystal, which uses structural symmetry breaking to produce two high-frequency mechanical resonances coupled to an optical cavity. The exceptional point is reached within a stable operating window below the phonon-lasing threshold, and we observe asymmetric redistribution of optomechanical damping and anti-damping between hybridized modes. These results establish diamond optomechanical crystals as a platform for non-Hermitian optomechanics, opening routes to topological mechanical dynamics in hybrid spin-phonon interfaces.
summary: This paper reports the first experimental access to an exceptional point in a diamond optomechanical crystal. By using engineered structural asymmetry, the authors demonstrate how to tune mechanical mode hybridization and redistribute optomechanical damping. This provides a new platform for non-Hermitian physics and topological dynamics in diamond-based quantum technologies.
main_problem: The experimental achievement and utilization of exceptional points (EPs) in diamond optomechanical systems to leverage non-Hermitian phenomena like chiral dynamics and topological state transfer.
main_result: The researchers successfully reached an exceptional point in a diamond optomechanical crystal by using structural symmetry breaking to couple two mechanical modes, observing asymmetric redistribution of damping and branch-switching topology.
method: The study uses structural asymmetry (sidewall angles) to mix mechanical modes and employs a fiber taper for evanescent coupling, with data analysis involving coupled-mode model fitting and COMSOL simulations.
model_or_system: A diamond optomechanical crystal (OMC) nanobeam where a single optical cavity mode is coupled to two high-frequency mechanical resonances (breathing and flexural modes) via radiation pressure and structural symmetry breaking.
key_observables: Mechanical power spectral density (PSD), mechanical eigenfrequencies, damping rates, and optical transmission spectra.
important_parameters: Optical wavelength ~1565 nm, mechanical frequencies ~10.1 GHz, optomechanical coupling rates ~290 kHz, and the transition from weak coupling to the exceptional point and phonon-lasing threshold.
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes a strong coherent intracarray field, neglects small radiation-pressure-induced cavity resonance shifts, and approximates mechanical frequency shifts as linear with photon number.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup and conceptual EP evolution; Figure 2 displays the SEM image and simulated mode profiles; Figure 3 illustrates Bloch-sphere trajectories and stability maps; Figure 4 shows the 2D map of eigenfrequencies.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of EPs in optomechanics, describes the diamond platform and fabrication-induced symmetry breaking, presents the experimental observation of the EP and its topological signatures, and discusses the implications for tunable spin-mechanical coupling.
why_interesting: It demonstrates experimental control over non-Hermitian singularities and eigenvector rotation, which is highly relevant for developing direction-dependent quantum state transfer and topological protection in hybrid quantum networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Exceptional points in multimode cavity optomechanics. (a) Fabry–Perot representation of a multimode optomechanical system, where two mechanical resonators with frequencies Ω1,2 modulate a common optical resonance ωc. (b) Coupling diagram for two mechanical modes ˆb1 and ˆb2 coupled to an optical mode ˆa with vacuum optomechanical coupling rates g1 and g2. The mechanical dissipation rates are γ1 and γ2, and the optical decay rate is κ = κex + κi. (c) Diamond optomechanical crystal (OMC) implementation. A fiber taper evanescently couples laser light into and out of the optical cavity, allowing the thermomechanical spectrum to be measured from the transmitted field. (d,e) Conceptual...

Figure 2. Symmetry-mixed mechanical modes in a diamond optomechanical crystal. (a) Scanning electron micrograph of the fabricated diamond optomechanical crystal (OMC) nanobeam. (b,c) Schematic unit cell showing the sidewall angles θw and θh, which break vertical reflection symmetry. (d,e) Mode-composition diagrams showing a breathing- like cavity unit-cell mode combined with a flexural-like mirror unit-cell mode. The minus and plus signs denote the relative displacement phase of the two parent mode components, giving the out-of-phase and in-phase mixed modes shown in (f) and (g), respectively. (f,g) Simulated displacement profiles of the two symmetry-mixed mechanical modes in the full OMC...

Figure 3. Optomechanical tuning through the exceptional-point regime. (a) Mechanical power spectral density (PSD) versus laser detuning ∆for three input powers P1, P2 and P3. Top row, measured spectra; bottom row, spectra calculated from the fitted two-mode model. White curves show the predicted eigenfrequencies Ω±. Increasing input power strengthens the optically mediated coupling, drawing the frequency branches together and modifying their damping. (b) Extracted damping rates γ± (points) and model predictions (black curves) versus ∆. Colored dashed curves show the damping expected from the diagonal optical backaction terms alone, excluding the off-diagonal coupling g1g2χ. At P3, the...

Figure 4. Branch-point topology of the mechanical exceptional point. (a) Measured real eigenfrequencies Ω± (markers) and fitted two-mode model surface versus laser detuning ∆and input power Pin. The two frequency sheets approach and meet at the inferred EP (purple point). Beyond the EP, the continuously evolved eigenbranches reconnect onto the opposite frequency sheets, with the (±) labels following branch continuity rather than frequency ordering. (b) Mechanical power spectral densities (PSDs) along the two degeneracy contours shown in Fig. 3d. For Pin < PEP, spectra follow the damping-degeneracy contour γ+ = γ−, isolating the collapse of the real-frequency splitting. For Pin > PEP,...

Figure 5. Stability of the exceptional-point trajectory. (a) Stability map of the complex cavity susceptibility χ = Re[χ]+i Im[χ] for the same experimentally extracted parameters in the main text, with |∆Ω|/2π = 6.9 MHz and ¯γ = 8.8 MHz . Shaded regions indicate the unstable regions where Im[˜Ω±] > 0. The white and purple dotted lines show where Re[˜Ω+] = Re[˜Ω−] and Im[˜Ω+] = Im[˜Ω−], respectively. Their intersection defines the EP. The pink shaded lobe denotes the experimentally accessible values of χ(Pin, ∆) for the measured cavity coupling (κex, κ) and available laser power, showing that the EP lies within both the stable and accessible regime.
